source_log.source: CAF South Africa World Cup profile
source_log.note: Describes South Africa as organized, disciplined, and built on unity rather than individual stardom.
Confirms tenure, background, and tournament pragmatism.
Useful for identity, compactness, and team mentality under Broos.
Warm-up match context and coach reaction.
Broos frames the Mexico opener as a chance to carry the nation's hopes.

notes: Current phase is team_profile only; no score, group, champion, or betting output.
fan_summary_en: Broos has turned South Africa into a compact tournament team that do not need the ball to bother you; if they survive the first pressure wave, they can make the match ugly, tactical, and surprisingly dangerous on counters and set plays.

sample_summary.primary_window: 2025 qualification cycle through 2026 World Cup preparation
notes: Uses official SAFA, FIFA, and CAF material plus the existing local data packet.
Chance-creation micro detail remains partial until a larger structured sample is available.

coach_profile.summary: Broos has built South Africa into a highly organized tournament side. The public official profile repeatedly stresses discipline, unity, and fearlessness, but the tactical base is still pragmatic: compact block first, controlled aggression second, and selective transition attacks third.

team_shape.in_possession_shape: Usually a back four with cautious support from the fullbacks, midfielders positioned for second balls and long switches, and a front line prepared to attack space early rather than sustain elaborate possession.
team_shape.out_of_possession_shape: Often a compact 4-5-1 or 4-1-4-1 defensive picture with disciplined horizontal spacing.
role_asymmetry: The goalkeeper and center-backs are heavily responsible for calming pressure waves.
Midfield shape is chosen more for control, recovery, and tactical discipline than for artistic possession.

key_roles.note: The team's most visible defensive leader and emotional stabilizer.
Important for midfield intelligence, passing range, and set-piece quality.
Key pieces for width, transition release, and crossing or set-piece delivery support.
South Africa's striker role is less about glamour finishing and more about running, outlets, and making counterattacks live.
Brings calmness and game-reading value in heavier tournament moments.

attack.summary: South Africa are most comfortable attacking from compactness rather than from long sterile possession phases. Their offense comes alive through counters, wide releases, second balls, and dead-ball situations.
notes: The first goal is often to keep the game manageable before chasing expanded attacking volume.
Set pieces and long-range or second-phase attacks matter more than intricate central combinations.

defense.summary: South Africa defend with discipline, compactness, and goalkeeper leadership. They are built to survive long spells without the ball, though the cost can be limited open-play chance creation on the other side.
notes: Broos does not need constant high pressing to make the team competitive.
The defensive identity is more about denying central access and forcing uncomfortable game scripts.

set_pieces.attack: Set pieces are an important equalizer because they reduce the need for sustained open-play superiority.
set_pieces.defense: The basic structure looks disciplined, but a fuller taker-target map is still missing.

coach_tendencies.leading_adjustments: When ahead, South Africa are likely to protect spacing, defensive shape, and emotional discipline before seeking extra attacking risk.
coach_tendencies.trailing_adjustments: When trailing, Broos can add directness and more open-field running, but the team is still more likely to remain structurally honest than to become reckless.
coach_tendencies.summary: Broos is a classic tournament coach in this context: disciplined, opponent-aware, and more interested in match controllability than in cosmetic possession numbers.

attack_ceiling.note: South Africa can create dangerous moments, but their highest level is still more situational than constant.

defensive_floor.note: Compactness, Williams, and Broos' structure give them a respectable floor.

set_piece_attack.note: A practical support weapon, especially in tight matches.

tempo_preference.note: They prefer controlled, manageable rhythms unless transition moments invite more speed.

transition_attack.note: Counters and second balls are more important than complex possession chains.

transition_defense_risk.note: Risk is present if they are forced to chase and widen the match, but usually contained when the block stays intact.

key_player_dependency.note: Williams and Mokoena are especially important for keeping the team coherent.

market_relevant_flags.note: His presence materially affects South Africa's defensive floor.
He influences both transition resistance and dead-ball upside.
South Africa are more comfortable in low- to medium-event scripts than in stretched end-to-end games.

verification_questions.claim: South Africa's strongest identity is a compact tournament block rather than a proactive high-possession style.
verification_questions.why_challengeable: Needs broader event data across weaker-opponent samples where they may have had more of the ball.

verification_questions.claim: Williams is the single biggest defensive-floor player in the squad.
verification_questions.why_challengeable: Should be tested against samples where midfield protection was stronger or weaker than usual.

verification_questions.claim: South Africa's attack depends more on counters, second balls, and set pieces than on settled central creativity.
verification_questions.why_challengeable: A wider structured sample may show more controlled-possession phases against lower blocks.

data.completion_note: Deep version completed to a usable handoff level, but packet remains partial pending fuller structured sample work and a complete set-piece map.
